title: Bills show a life worth running
subtitle: A short affirmation for shifting bills from dread to evidence.
excerpt: A short affirmation for the dread of bills arriving. Each statement is evidence of a life running: a home with power and heat, a phone with a signal, a broadband connection, food delivered, services used. This affirmation reframes the statement itself before the resentment of paying it can land.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 7 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Invite peace around paying bills." The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).

content.text: A short affirmation for the dread that comes when a bill arrives. Each statement is evidence: the electricity bill shows a home with power, the broadband bill shows a working connection, the phone bill shows a signal. They are arriving because a life is running. This affirmation reinstates that frame before the resentment can.

Bills arrive because services have been used. The electricity kept the lights on, the heating ran, the broadband connected the work. Receiving a bill is the moment those services are made visible, a ledger of what the month contained. The affirmation holds that frame at the moment the resentment is most likely to land.

attrs.quote: Bills show a life worth running.

content.text: Three times, slow, at the moment the bill arrives. When the email lands, when the letter arrives on the mat, when the direct debit notification appears. Say it out loud if possible. Let the words settle before you open the bill and read the amount.
Use this affirmation consistently through one bill-heavy period, when statements are arriving in clusters, rather than switching between different ones. The wearing-in is the point.
If the resentment is already loud and the affirmation can't land, tap first.
works on the dread of opening them. For the moment of actually paying,
gives each payment a deliberate one-breath moment before you click send.

Adapted from Day 7 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program by Rebecca J Page, where the focus is inviting peace around paying bills. The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive, repeated over time) follows long-standing affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement and modern-form work by writers such as Louise Hay. See the sources note for the dates.
